Facts.text: Stephan I succeeds Lucius I as Catholic Pope
Duke Henry of Saxony becomes King Henry I of Oostfrankischreich
The Ostromir Gospel, now the oldest surviving Russian manuscript is recorded completed by its scribe Deacon Grigory
Battle at Mailberg: Vratislav II of Bohemia defeats Leopold II, Margrave of Austria
Construction of Santa Croce begins in Florence, the largest Franciscan church in the world (consecrated 1442)
Holy Roman Emperor Louis IV and assembly of priests select Pietro Rainalducc as anti-Pope Nicolas V; consecrated in Rome by the Bishop of Venice
Jodhpur, Sun City, founded by Rao Jodhpur in India
Battle at Biblingen: Zwabische Union beats rebel Wurttemberg farmers
German Duchy of Wurttemberg becomes Lutheran under Ulrich, Duke of Württemberg
San Marcos University in Lima, Peru, opens (oldest continuously operating university in the Americas)
Catholic League under duke Henri de Guise occupies Paris
Spanish garrison of Aardenburg surrenders to Mauritius
Emperor Shah Jahan commissions construction of the Red Fort at Shahjahanabad, Mughal Empire (now Dehli, India)
England & Netherlands form League of Augsburg
English KingWilliam IIIdeparts to Netherlands
Maria Theresa crowned Queen of Bohemia in Prague
French First Minister of State and Controller-General of Finances Anne Robert Jacques Turgot resigns after being ordered to do so
1st ice cream advertisement (Philip Lenzi in the NY Gazette)
Ratified copies of the Treaty of Paris, ending the Revolutionary War exchanged between US and Great Britain, in Paris
Society of St Tammany is formed by Revolutionary War soldiers. It later becomes an infamous group of NYC political bosses
Nore Mutiny: British Royal Navy sailors mutiny on the Thames, England and blockade London
The first major battle of the Greek War of Independence against the Turks occurs in Valtetsi
Gaetano Donizetti's opera "L'elisir d'amore" premieres in Milan
Federal troops occupies Baton Rouge, Louisiana
Battle of Raymond, Mississippi
Battle of Drewry's Bluff, fought in Chesterfield County, Virginia (Peninsula Campaign), Confederate victory (US Civil War)
Battle of Spotsylvania, fought near Courthouse, Virginia (Overland Campaign), inconclusive result (US Civil War)
Battle of Todd's Tavern, cavalry battle fought near Todds Tavern, Virginia (Sheridan's Raid), inconclusive result (US Civil War)
US Union colonel Emory Upton (24) promoted to brigadier general
Last land action of Civil war at Palmito Ranch, Texas
Royal assent passes Manitoba Act of 1870, approving Manitoba becoming a province of Canada
Segregated street cars integrated in Louisville, Kentucky
US Assay Office in Helena, Montana, authorized
Ottawa Rough Riders 1st outside competition vs Britannia
Treaty of Bardo, Tunisia becomes a French protectorate
Crouching start first used in track and field by Charles Sherrill of Yale
Louisiana legalizes prize fighting
Riot against tax increase in Paramaribo, Suriname
Ludwig Englander's musical "Passing Show" premieres in NYC
1800-1900 year old fossil of "girl of Yde" found in Drente, Netherlands
Battle at Thessalie: Turkish army beats Greece
Louisiana adopts new constitution with "grandfather clause" designed to eliminate black voters
Boer attack under Sarel Eloff, about 70 killed
Lord Roberts' British forces occupy Crown city during Boer War
US President McKinley visits San Francisco
140,000 miners of anthracite coal in Pennsylvania go out on a strike called by the United Mine Workers after the owners have refused to recognize the UMW, negotiate or submit to arbitration
Wireless Radio Broadcasting is patented by Nathan B. Stubblefield
Philadelphia Athletics pitcher Charles "Chief" Bender no-hits Cleveland Naps, 4-0
1st Inter-Empire Championships open in London
American economic expert W. Morgan Schuster arrives by invitation to assume almost dictatorial power over Persia's finances; a move resented by Russia
English runner Harry Green runs world record marathon 2:38:16.2 in the Polytechnic Marathon in London, England
Croatians plunder Armenia, killing 250
Franklin K. Mathiews first presents idea of "Book Week"
James Connolly and Sean McDermott are executed following their involvement in the Easter Rising in Dublin
New York Yankees and Washington Senators play second straight extra inning tie, 4-4 in 15 innings at the Polo Grounds; 0-0 in 12 the previous day

The Transvaal British Indian Association calls a mass meeting to organise opposition to the proposed Asiatics (Land and Trading) Amendment Act; in the Act, Transvaal Indians are prohibited from owning shares in limited companies
National Hospital Day 1st observed in the United States
Uzbekistan and Kyrgyzstan become autonomous Soviet republics
British General Strike ends after 9 days, coal mine workers stay on strike
Second Opium Law introduced - international law to control drugs
Pulitzer prize awarded to Julia Peterkin (Scarlet Sister Mary)
Federal Emergency Relief Administration & Agricultural Adjustment Administration form to help the needy & farmers
Ralph Vaughan Williams' opera "Poisoned Kiss" premieres in London
St Louis Cards beat Philadelphia Phillies, 15-3
Sandoz Labs begins manufacturing LSD (lysergic acid diethylamide)
French marines occupy St Maarten
German tanks conquer Moerdijk bridges, Netherlands
Nazi blitzkrieg and conquest of France begins with German forces crossing the Muese River
British forces march into Alexandria, Egypt
Konrad Zuse presents the Z3, the world's first working programmable, fully automatic computer, in Berlin
1,500 Jews gassed in Auschwitz
Nazi U-boat sinks American cargo ship at mouth of Mississippi River
Russia occupies Crackow, until Aug 23, 1943
Axis forces in North Africa surrender
German troops in Tunisia, North Africa, surrender
900+ 8th US Air Force bombers attack Zwikau, Bohlen & Brux
Crimea purged of Nazi troops
German police arrest Dutch resistance member Gerrit van der Veen and later execute him
USSR lifts blockade of West Berlin after US, UK, and allies successfully supply the city during the Berlin Airlift
Charlton Playground named in Bronx
Gaj Singh is crowned Maharaja of Jodhpur at the age of four after the death of his father Hanwant Singh in a plane crash
KUHT TV channel 8 (PBS) in Houston, TX, begins broadcasting
Chicago Cub Sam "Toothpick" Jones is 1st African-American to pitch a no-hitter, winning 4-0 over Pittsburgh Pirates, at Wrigley Field, Chicago
Brooklyn Dodgers pitcher Carl Erskine's throws 2nd career no-hitter, beats NY Giants, 3-0, at Ebbetts Field in Brooklyn, NYC
East Pakistan struck by cyclone and tidal waves
"Nee Nee Na Na Na Na Nu Nu" by Dicky Doo & The Don'ts hits #40
US & Canada form North American Air Defense Command (NORAD)
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Enewetak Atoll in the Marshall Islands
Grevelingen dam closes in major project to protect the southwest of the Netherlands from flooding
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Christmas Island
Race riot in Birmingham, Alabama
Italian diplomat and politician Manlio Brosio chosen as secretary-general of NATO
Israel and West Germany establish diplomatic relations with an exchange of letters
St Louis' Busch Memorial Stadium opens, Braves lose to Cards 4-3 in 12 innings
20th Cannes Film Festival: "Blowup" directed by Michelangelo Antonioni wins the Grand Prix du Festival International du Film
H. Rap Brown replaces Stokely Carmichael as chairman of Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC)
WSKG TV channel 46 (PBS) in Binghamton, NY, begins broadcasting
Pilot Ken Wallis achieves record speed for autogyros - 179 KPH
KTVM TV channel 6 in Butte, MT (NBC/ABC) begins broadcasting
Race riots in Augusta Georgia; 6 blacks killed (5 by cops)
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Milwaukee Brewers beat Minnesota Twins, 4-3, in 22 innings (completed 5/13)
"Dueling Tubas" novelty single by Martin Mull hits #92
US merchant ship Mayaguez seized by Cambodian forces
US Commerce Department says hurricane names will no longer be only female
1st non-stop trans-North American balloon flight lands at Sainte-Félicité, Quebec. Maxie Anderson and son Kristian piloted the Kitty Hawk for five days.
NY Islanders tie their own record with two shorthanded playoff goals in a period vs Minnesota North Stars
Pulitzer Prize for Fiction is awarded to John Updike for novel 'Rabbit Is Rich'
US Football League forms
Discovery moves to Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center (Florida) for mating of STS 41-D
France performs nuclear test
Joe Lucius scores his 13th hole-in-one on same hole
White Horse by Laid Back hits #26
World of Rivers world exposition opens in New Orleans
Amy Eilberg is ordained in NY as 1st woman Conservative rabbi
Fred Markham (US), unpaced & unaided by wind, is 1st to pedal 65 mph on a level course, Big Sand Flat, California
"Entertainment Tonight" makes its 2,000th TV performance
Last graffiti covered NYC subway car retired
Retired British pilot Jack Mann is kidnapped by Islamic fundamentalists
Comic Relief USA '90 (4th one) raises $4.7 million
A new cancer drug is announced which can only be found in bark of a rare tree in the Pacific Northwest
1st Belgian woman (Ingrid Baeyens) to ascend Mount Everest
Final episode of 6 year run of ABC's "Wonder Years" in Netherlands
WIBC Bowling Queens won by Anne Marie Duggan
Dow Jones Industrial Average sets a new record for 5th straight day of the week (4430.59)
Jose Mesa gets 1st of his M.L. record 37 consecutive saves
Yankees losing 8-0 to Chicago White Sox, come back to win 9-8
14 North Koreans defect to South Korea
Angels scores 13 in 7th vs White Sox
Australian Susie Maroney becomes first to swim from Cuba to Florida
Russia & Chechnya sign peace deal after 400 years of conflict
Tornado narrowly misses downtown Miami
David Steel becomes the first Presiding Officer (speaker) of the modern Scottish Parliament
Tate Modern art gallery opens in the redesigned Bankside Power Station by Herzog & de Meuron in London, now the world's most visited contemporary art museum
Ringo Starr's seventh All-Starr Band debuts in concert; members include: Roger Hodgson, Ian Hunter, Howard Jones, Greg Lake, Sheila E., and Mark Rivera
Fifty-nine Democratic lawmakers bring the Texas Legislature to a standstill by going into hiding in a dispute over a Republican congressional redistricting plan
The Riyadh compound bombings, carried out by Al Qaeda, 26 killed

A major rebellion occurs in São Paulo as members of criminal organization Primeiro Comando da Capital attack police officers and stations, eventually escalating to several prisons in Brazil leaving around 130 dead
Riots in Karachi, Pakistan are started by the arrival at Karachi's airport of independently minded Chief Justice Iftikhar Muhammad Chaudhry and go on to kill 48 people
Wenchuan earthquake, measuring 7.8 in magnitude occurs in Sichuan, China, killing over 87,000, injuring 374,643 and leaving homeless between 4.8 million and 11 million people
An Afriqiyah Airways Flight crashes and kills everyone but one person on board
The discovery of a missing Mayan calendar piece disproves 2012 Armageddon
Paris Saint-Germain win the Ligue 1 French football title
Voters in Eastern Ukraine vote for self-rule in referendum
Brazil declares end to Zika national emergency as cases decrease
Insurgent group attacks the town of Muse, Myanmar, killing at least 19 people, Ta’ang National Liberation Army claim responsibility
Cuba announced more rationing of products such as rice and beans due to US trade embargo and hoarding
Gunman kill five people and a priest in a Catholic church in Dablo, Burkina Faso, before setting fire to and looting nearby buildings
Manchester City beats Brighton, 4-1 to claim back-to-back English Premier League titles with 98 points, 1 ahead of runners-up, Liverpool
Militants storm a hospital and a funeral in Kabul, Afghanistan, shooting an estimated 40 people dead including new mothers and their babies
US coronavirus task force testifies to the Senate that the US "is not out of the woods yet" that the pandemic is not under control and risks surge in cases if reopens too soon
Wuhan, epicenter of the COVID-19 outbreak, draws up plans to test all 11 million residents after it records a new cluster of 6 cases
"Fat Ham" an adaption of William Shakespeare's Hamlet by James Ijames opens off-Broadway at The Public Theater in New York (Pulitzer Prize for Drama 2022)
First images published of the supermassive blackhole Sagittarius A* that lies at the heart of the Milky Way captured by the Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration

North Korea orders a national lockdown after confirming its first COVID-19 cases with the Omicron variant. International experts doubt these the first, express concern for its unvaccinated 25 million population
Very early Californian wildfire called "a dangerous new normal" by local fire chief after 24 houses destroyed and 900 homes evacuated in Laguna Niguel, Orange County
US pandemic-era expulsion policy Title 42, which expelled most migrants, expires
Flash flood kills at least 300 people in northern Afghanistan and destroys nearly 2000 homes
US and China agree to a 90-day trade deal in which tariffs on most Chinese imports will drop from 145% to 30%, and tariffs on US imports will drop from 125% to 10%
Revolt of Catalonia begins, an uprising against the Spanish KingPhilip IVin eastern Spain
Swedish botanistCarl Linnaeussets off from Uppsala on a six-month journey to Lapland
American GeneralBenjamin Lincolnsurrenders Charleston to the British (Revolutionary War)
First Coalition:NapoleonI of France conquers Venice
Charles Darwinvisits Alexander Caldcleugh’s copper mines at Panuncillo in northern Chile and is impressed by the extraordinary feats of labor performed by the workers
Oscar IIFredrik is crowned King of Sweden
Battle of Batoche:Louis Rieland the Metis defeated by Frederick Middleton, leads to collapse of Provincial Government of Saskatchewan and surrender of Riel
George Bernard Shaw's play "Getting Married" premieres in London
Second NAACP conference in NYC
French priest Jean Vianney (1786-1859) canonized as Saint John Vianney by PopePius XI
Airship Norge is the first vessel to fly over North Pole, led by Norwegian explorerRoald Amundsenand piloted by the craft's Italian designer Umberto Nobile
Dmitri Shostakovich's 1st Symphony premieres in Leningrad
GeneralJózef Piłsudskireturns to power in Poland after coup d'état against the Witos regime
German ChancellorHans Lutherresigns for the second time after a no-confidence vote against his minority government
Benito Mussoliniannounces moves to end women's suffrage in a speech to the Senate in Italy
Pulitzer Prize for Drama awarded toMarc Connellyfor "Green Pastures)"
Body of kidnapped son ofCharles Lindberghis found in Hopewell, New Jersey
"Cocktails For Two" byDuke Ellingtonhits #1
Coronation of KingGeorge VIof Great Britain (and his other realms and territories beyond the sea) at Westminster Abbey, London
Arthur Honeggerand Paul Claudel's oratorio "Jeanne d'Arc au bûcher" (Joan of Arcat the Stake) premieres in Basel, Switzerland
Martin BormannsucceedsRudolf HessasAdolf Hitler's deputy
David Ben-Gurionleaves Jewish state in Palestine
British premierWinston Churchillarrives in USA
QueenWilhelmina of the Netherlandsabdicates due to ill health
First foreign woman ambassador received in USA,Vijaya Lakshmi Panditfrom India
Darius Milhaud's opera "Bolivar" premieres in Paris
Elvis Presleyappears on a Frank Sinatra television special;Elvissings Sinatra's 1957Cy Colemanhit "Witchcraft", and Frank performs Presley's 1956 classic "Love Me Tender"
RussianMikhail Botvinnikwins World Chess Championship for his third time
Bob Dylanwalks out of "TheEd SullivanShow" over a dispute about his song choice
The Rolling Stonesrecord their smash hit single "(I Can't Get No) Satisfaction"
1st quadraphonic concert byPink Floydat the Games for May concert in London
Columbia Pictures releases "Good Times" a musical comedy western starringSonny & Cherand George Sanders;Cher's 1st starring role is also the feature debut of director William Friedkin
Track Records releases "Are You Experienced" album by theJimi HendrixExperience - one of the most influential debut albums ever
"March of the Poor" under RevRalph Abernathyreaches Washington, D.C.
Ernie Bankshits his 500th home run
Paul McCartney& Wings release single "Mary Had a Little Lamb"
Chris Evert's 125-match winning streak on clay comes to an end
In Fatima Portugal, a Spanish Traditionalist priest who opposed Vatican II reforms and saw PopeJohn Paul IIas a perpetrator of them, is stopped prior to his attempt to attack the Pope with a bayonet
South African prisonerNelson Mandelasees his wifeWinnie Mandelafor the 1st time in 22 years
President Reaganappoints Dr James C. Fletcher NASA Administrator
Nora Dunn and Sinéad O'Connor boycott "Saturday Night Live" to protestAndrew Dice Clay's hosting which was perceived as sexist; the show uses a time delay for the third time in its history
"Pulp Fiction", directed byQuentin Tarantinoand starringJohn Travolta,Uma ThurmanandSamuel L. Jacksonpremieres at the Cannes Film Festival (Palme d'Or 1994)
Martin Brodeurties NHL record getting his 3rd playoff shutout in 4
"It's Your Love" single released by Time McGraw andFaith Hill
Ringo Starr's sixth All-Starr Band debuts in concert; members include: Todd Rundgren, Jack Bruce, Gary Brooker, Simon Kirke, and Timmy Cappello

Former US PresidentJimmy Carterarrives in Cuba for a five-day visit withFidel Castro, becoming the first President of the United States, in or out of office, to visit the island since Castro's 1959 revolution.
American songwritersBurt Bacharachand Hal David receive Library of Congress Gershwin Prize at the White House in Washington, D.C.
Pope Franciscanonizes the 813 martyrs of Otranto as Saints
Michel Temer, former vice-president, becomes Brazilian interim President after a vote to impeachDilma Rousseff
FormerOne DirectionsingerHarry Stylesreleases his self-titled debut solo album
Indian Prime MinisterNarendra Modiannounces a $260 million economic rescue package, worth 10% of GDP
Russia's confirmed cases of COVID-19 reach 232,000, 2nd highest in the world, a day after PresidentVladimir Putineased the country's lockdown
US Republicans vote to demote their number three Liz Cheney from party leadership after she publicly rebukedDonald Trumpfor lies about the election
Elon Muskannounces NBCUniversial executive Linda Yaccarino will replace him as CEO of Twitter
Rock bandFoo Fightersfire drummer Josh Freese after two years with the group
English barons serve an ultimatum onKing Johnwhich eventually leads to the creation and signing of the Magna Carta
Dutch province Drenthe adopts the Gregorian calendar, making the day before in Drenthe April 29, 1701
William Wilberforcemakes his first major speech on abolition in the UK House of Commons, reasoning the slave trade morally reprehensible and an issue of natural justice
Shōkō, 101st Emperor of Japan (1412-28), born in Japan (d. 1428)
Cosimo II de' Medici, Grand Duke of Tuscany (1609-21), born in Palazzo Pitti, Florence (d. 1621)
Louis Hennepin, Flemish missionary who explorer the interior of North America, born in Ath, Spanish Netherlands (d. 1705)
Jan Frans van Bloemen, Flemish landscape painter, born in Antwerp (d. 1749) baptism date
August II the Strong, Elector of Saxony, King of Poland and Grand Duke of Lithuania (claimed to have sired over 350 children), born in Dresden, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1733)
Giovanni Antonio Ricieri, Italian composer and castrato singer, born in Venice, Republic of Venice (d. 1746)
Luigi Vanvitelli, Italian architect (Palace of Caserta), born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (now Italy) (d. 1773)
Louis Philippe I, Duke of Orléans, French Prince of the Bourbon House, born at the Palace of Versailles, France (d. 1785)
Johann Baptist Wanhal [Jan Křtitel Vaňhal], Czech organist, cellist, composer, and teacher, born in Nechanice, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1813)
Gaetano Manna, Italian composer, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1804)
Giovanni Battista Viotti, Italian violonist and composer, born in Fontanetto Po, Savoia, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1824)
Franz Anton Hoffmeister, German composer,  and nusic publisher, born in Rottenburg am Neckar, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1812)
Manuel de Godoy 'Prince of the Peace', Spanish statesman, 1st Secretary of State of Spain, born in Badajoz, Spain (d. 1851)
[Johannes] Carsten Hauch, Danish poet and writer, born in Frederikshald, Norway (d. 1872)
Johann Hermann Kufferath, German composer, born in Mülheim an der Ruhr, Duchy of Westphalia (d. 1864)
Justus von Liebig, German agricultural and biological chemist who is considered the founder of organic chemistry, born in Hesse-Darmstadt, Germany (d. 1873)
Robert Baldwin, Canadian politician (popularizer of responsible government in Canada), born in York, Upper Canada (d. 1858)
Amos Beebe Eaton, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Catskill, New York (d. 1877)
Johan Vilhelm Snellman, Finnish journalist, statesman and nationalist, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1881)
Edward Lear, English landscape painter and poet (Complete Nonsense Book), born in London, England (d. 1888)
Louis Ludwig Blenker, German-American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Worms, Germany (d. 1863)
Johann Czerski, German clergyman, founded German Catholicism, born in Warlubien, West Prussia (d. 1893)
Gabriel Dante Rossetti, English Pre-Raphaelite figure poet and painter, born in London, England (d. 1882)
Pavlos Carrer, Greek composer (Isabella d'Aspeno), born in Zakynthos, Greece (d. 1896)
Gabriel Fauré, French organist, composer (Requiem; Ballade), and educator (Paris Conservatoire, 1905-20), born in Pamiers, France (d. 1924)
Cyrill Kistler, German composer, educator, and music theorist, born in Großaitingen, Swabia, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1907)
Henry Cabot Lodge, U.S. statesman, born in Beverly, Massachusetts (d. 1924)
Joseph Toole, American politician (1st Governor of Montana, 1899-93 & 1901-08), born in Savannah, Missouri (d. 1929)
Anatoly Lyadov, Russian composer (From the Apocalypse; Bewitched Lake), conductor, and pedagogue (St. Petersburg Conservatory, 1878-1914), born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1914)
Ivan Caryll [Félix Tilkin], Belgian composer of operetta and musical comedy (The Shop Girl; The Spring Chicken; The Pink Lady), born in Liège, Belgium (d. 1921)
Charles Bordes, French composer, born in La Roche-Corbon, France (d. 1909)
Lafayette "Lave" Cross [Vratislav Kriz], American MLB baseball infielder, 1892-1907 (Philadelphia Phillies, Philadelphia Athletics,  and 5 other teams), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 1927)
Hugh Trumble, Australian cricket spin bowler and captain (32 Tests, 141 wickets; 2 x Test hat-tricks), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1938)
Halsey William Wilson, American publisher of reference books (Readers' Guide, Cumulative Book Index, Book Review Digest), born in Wilmington, Vermont (d. 1954)
Clemens von Pirquet, Austrian physician (Pirquet’s skin test for tuberculosis, coined the word 'allergy'), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1929)
Prince Harald of Schleswig-Holstein, grandson of English queen Victoria, born in Cumberland Lodge, United Kingdom (d. 1876 at 8 days old)
Lincoln Ellsworth, American polar explorer, led 1st transarctic, transantarctic flights, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1951)
Kyrylo Stetsenko, Ukrainian composer (Burlaka), choral conductor, music critic, teacher, and later, Ukrainian Orthodox priest, born in Kvitkiv, Russian Empire (d. 1922)
Albert Saverys, Flemish painter (Winter in Flanders, The Poppies), born in Deinze, Belgium (d. 1964)[1]
Hermann Grabner, Austrian composer (Die Richterin), born in Graz, Austria (d. 1969)
Peter Scharow, Russian-Italian actor and director (Chechov), born in Perm, Russia (d. 1969)
Nandor Zsolt, Hungarian composer, born in Esztergom, Hungary (d. 1936)
Otto Frank, German born father of Anne Frank who saw her diaries published, born in Frankfurt (d. 1980)
Fritz Kortner [Fritz Nathan Kohn], Austrian character actor (Pandora's Box) and director (A Girl You Don't Forget), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1970)
Cleo Ridgely, American silent screen actress (I Remember Mama), born in New York City (d. 1962)
Donald Knight, English cricket batsman (2 Tests; Surrey CCC, Oxford University CC), born in Sutton, England (d. 1960)
Indra Devi [Eugenie Peterson], Latvian yoga instructor known as "The First Lady of Yoga", born in Riga, Latvia (d. 2002)
Helene Weigel, Austrian-German actress (Metropolis, Mutter Courage), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1971)
Hyam Greenbaum, British violinist, composer and conductor of the 1st TV orchestra (BBC), born in England (d. 1942)
Lennox R. F. Berkeley, British composer and pedagogue (Castaway), born in Boars Hill, England (d. 1989)
Wilfrid Hyde-White, British actor (My Fair Lady, Peyton Place), born in Gloucestershire, England (d. 1991)
Maurice Ewing, US pioneering geologist and geophysicist, born in Lockney, Texas (d. 1974)
Leslie Charteris, Chinese English American detective writer (Simon Templar character in The Saint), born in Singapore (d. 1993)
Frank DeCaires, West Indian cricket batsman (3 Tests, 2 x 50; British Guiana), born in British Guiana (d. 1959)
Charles B. Fulton, American jurist and chief judge of the Southern District of Florida (1966-77), born in Fallon, Nevada (d. 1996)
Gordon Jenkins, American arranger and orchestra leader (Nat King Cole, Frank Sinatra), born in Webster Groves, Missouri (d. 1984)
Marshal Royal, American jazz alto saxophonist and clarinetist (Count Basie, 1952-70), born in Sapulpa, Oklahoma (d.1995)[1]
Bertus Aafjes, Dutch poet and writer, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Dan Daniel, American politician (Rep-D-VA, 1969-88), born in Chatham, Virginia (d. 1988)
Howard K. Smith, American TV newsman and journalist (ABC, Moderated Kennedy-Nixon debate), born in Ferriday, Louisiana (d. 2002)
Mary Kay Ash, American businesswoman and founder of Mary Kay Cosmetics, born in Hot Wells, Texas (d. 2001)
Ellis Kohs, American composer (Lohiau and Hiiaka), educator, and musicologist, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2000)
Andries Antonie Rieu, Dutch conductor (Maastricht Symphony Orchestra, 1949-80), born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1992)
Oscar Beregi Jr, Hungarian actor (Young Frankenstein, Panic in City), born in Budapest, Austria-Hungary (d. 1976)
(Otis W.) "Joe" Maphis, American session and country music guitarist ("Dim Lights"), born in Suffolk, Virginia (d. 1986)
Edvard Mirzoyan, Armenian composer, born in Gori, Georgia (d. 2012)
Farley Mowat, Canadian writer and naturalist, born in Belleville, Ontario, (d. 2014)
Giovanni Benelli, Italian archbishop of Florence and Papal candidate, born in Vernio, Italy (d. 1982)
Joseph Beuys, German avant-garde painter (Fluxus, movement), born in Krefeld, Germany (d. 1986)
Barend Busnac, Dutch office clerk and resistance fighter, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1944)
Marco Denevi, Argentine writer (Rosaura at Ten in the Morning, Secret Ceremony), born in Saenz Pena, Argentina (d. 1998)
Ewart Abner, American record company executive (President of Motown, manager of Stevie Wonder), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1997)
Giovanni Testori, Italian writer (Rocco e i suoi fratelli), born in Milan, Italy (d. 1993)
Hector Laing, Baron Laing of Dunphail, Scottish biscuit manufacturer (McVitie & Price), pilot and Director of the Bank of England (1973-91), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 2010)
Alexander Esenin-Volpin, Russian mathematician (ultrafinitism) and human rights activist, born in Leningrad, Soviet Union (d. 2016)
John Simon, American author, literary and theater critic (NY Times), born in Subotica, Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes (d. 2019)
Earl Hutto, American politician (Rep-D-Florida, 1979-95), born in Panama City, Florida (d. 2020)
Mervyn Dymally, American politician (Rep-D-CA, 1981-93), born in Cedros, Trinidad and Tobago (d. 2012)
Philip Springer, American composer (“Santa Baby”; "The Next Time"; "The Bells Of Notre Dame"), born in New York City[1]
Barbara Dane [Spillman], American folk, blues, jazz, and protest song singer, born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2024)[1]
Henry Cosby, American songwriter, musician and producer for Motown ("Tears of a Clown"; "For Once In My Life"), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2002)
Ivan Král, Czech-American bass player, songwriter, record producer (Patti Smith; Iggy Pop; John Waite), born in Prague, Czechoslovakia (d. 2020)
Manuel Lujan Jr, American politician (Rep-R-NM, 1969-89), born in San Ildefonso Pueblo, New Mexico (d. 2019)
Dollard St. Laurent, Canadian ice hockey player, born in Verdun, Quebec (d. 2015)
Sam Nujoma, Namibian revolutionary and politician, first President of Namibia (1990-2005), born in Ongandjera, Soutwest Africa (d. 2025)[1]
Mariya Shubina, Russian canoeist (Olympic gold USSR K-2 500m 1960; World C'ship gold x 4), born in Protasovo, Russia (d. 2025)
Pat McCormick, American diver (4 x Olympic gold women's springboard & platform 1952, 56), born in Seal Beach, California (d. 2023)
Johan Fleerackers, Flemish civil servant and cultural attaché (Minister of Cutlure, 1965-77), born in Mortsel, Belgium (d. 1989)
(Antoni) "Ton" Hartsuiker, Dutch pianist, composer, broadcaster, and educator, born in Zwolle, Netherlands (d. 2015)
Andrey Voznesensky, Russian poet, born in Moscow, Soviet Union (d. 2010)
Elechi Amadi, Nigerian novelist (The Concubine), born in Aluu (d. 2016)
Felipe Alou, Dominican baseball utility (MLB All-Star 1962, 66, 68; SF Giants, Milwaukee/Atlanta Braves) and manager (Montreal Expos 1992–2001, SF Giants 2003–06), born in Haina, Dominican Republic
Gary Peacock, American jazz bassist (Keith Jarrett Trio), born in Burley, Idaho (d. 2020)
Johnny Bucyk, Canadian ice hockey player, born in Edmonton, Alberta
David Snell, British jazz harpist and conductor, born in Grantham, Lincolnshire, England
Frank Stella, American artist, born in Malden, Massachusetts[1]
Guillermo Endara, President of Panama (1989-94), born in Panama City, Panama (d. 2009)
Klaus Doldinger, German jazz saxophonist (Passport) and composer for film, born in Berlin, Germany
George Carlin, American comedian (7 dirty words, AM & FM, Carwash), born in The Bronx, New York (d. 2008)
